readme.text:
# PDD (Prompt-Driven Development) Command Line Interface ![PDD-CLI Version](https://img.shields.io/badge/pdd--cli-v0.0.179-blue) [![Discord](https://img.shields.io/badge/Discord-join%20chat-7289DA.svg?logo=discord&logoColor=white)](https://discord.gg/Yp4RTh8bG7) ## Introduction PDD (Prompt-Driven Development) is a toolkit for AI-powered code generation and maintenance. **Getting started is simple:** ```bash # Install and run uv tool install pdd-cli pdd setup pdd connect ``` This launches a web interface at `localhost:9876` where you can: - Implement GitHub issues automatically - Generate and test code from prompts - Manage your PDD projects visually

For CLI users, PDD also offers powerful **agentic commands** that implement GitHub issues automatically: - `pdd change ` - Implement feature requests (12-step workflow) - `pdd bug ` - Create failing tests for bugs - `pdd fix ` - Fix the failing tests - `pdd generate ` - Generate architecture.json from a PRD issue (11-step workflow) - `pdd test ` - Generate UI tests from issue descriptions (18-step workflow with exploratory testing, contract validation, accessibility audits) For prompt-based workflows, the **`sync`** command automates the complete development cycle with intelligent decision-making, real-time visual feedback, and sophisticated state management. ## Whitepaper For a detailed explanation of the concepts, architecture, and benefits of Prompt-Driven Development, please refer to our full whitepaper. This document provides an in-depth look at the PDD philosophy, its advantages over traditional development, and includes benchmarks and case studies. [Read the Full Whitepaper with Benchmarks](docs/whitepaper_with_benchmarks/whitepaper_w_benchmarks.md) Also see the Prompt‑Driven Development Doctrine for core principles and practices: [docs/prompt-driven-development-doctrine.md](docs/prompt-driven-development-doctrine.md) ## Installation ### Prerequisites for macOS On macOS, you'll need to install some prerequisites before installing PDD: 1. **Install Xcode Command Line Tools** (required for Python compilation): ```bash xcode-select --install ``` 2. **Install Homebrew** (recommended package manager for macOS): ```bash /bin/bash -c "$(curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Homebrew/install/HEAD/install.sh)" ``` After installation, add Homebrew to your PATH: ```bash echo 'eval "$(/opt/homebrew/bin/brew shellenv)"' >> ~/.zprofile && eval "$(/opt/homebrew/bin/brew shellenv)" ``` 3. **Install Python** (if not already installed): ```bash # Check if Python is installed python3 --version # If Python is not found, install it via Homebrew brew install python ``` **Note**: Recent versions of macOS no longer ship with Python pre-installed. PDD requires Python 3.8 or higher. The `brew install python` command installs the latest Python 3 version. ### Recommended Method: uv We recommend installing PDD using the [uv package manager](https://github.com/astral-sh/uv) for better dependency management and automatic environment configuration: ```bash # Install uv if you haven't already curl -LsSf https://astral.sh/uv/install.sh | sh # Install PDD using uv tool install uv tool install pdd-cli ``` This installation method ensures: - Faster installations with optimized dependency resolution - Automatic environment setup without manual configuration - Proper handling of the PDD_PATH environment variable - Better isolation from other Python packages The PDD CLI will be available immediately after installation without requiring any additional environment configuration. Verify installation: ```bash pdd --version ``` With the CLI on your `PATH`, continue with: ```bash pdd setup ``` The command detects agentic CLI tools, scans for API keys, configures models, and seeds local configuration files. If you postpone this step, the CLI detects the missing setup artifacts the first time you run another command and shows a reminder banner so you can complete it later (the banner is suppressed once `~/.pdd/api-env` exists or when your project already provides credentials via `.env` or `.pdd/`). ### Alternative: pip Installation If you prefer using pip, you can install PDD with: ```bash pip install pdd-cli ``` ## Advanced Installation Options ### Virtual Environment Installation ```bash # Create virtual environment python -m venv pdd-env # Activate environment # On Windows: pdd-env\Scripts\activate # On Unix/MacOS: source pdd-env/bin/activate # Install PDD pip install pdd-cli ``` ## Getting Started ### Option 1: Web Interface (Recommended) The easiest way to use PDD is through the web interface: ```bash # 1. Install PDD curl -LsSf https://astral.sh/uv/install.sh | sh uv tool install pdd-cli # 2. Run setup (API keys, shell completion) pdd setup # 3. Launch the web interface pdd connect ``` This opens a browser-based interface where you can: - **Run Commands**: Execute `pdd change`, `pdd bug`, `pdd fix`, `pdd sync` etc. visually - **Browse Files**: View and edit prompts, code, and tests in your project - **Remote Access**: Access your session from any browser via PDD Cloud (use `--local-only` to disable) ### Option 2: Issue-Driven CLI For CLI enthusiasts, implement GitHub issues directly: **Prerequisites:** 1. **GitHub CLI** - Required for issue access: ```bash brew install gh && gh auth login ``` 2. **One Agentic CLI** - Required to run the workflows (install at least one): - **Claude Code**: `npm install -g @anthropic-ai/claude-code` (requires `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY`) - **Gemini CLI**: `npm install -g @google/gemini-cli` (requires `GOOGLE_API_KEY` or `GEMINI_API_KEY`) - **Codex CLI**: `npm install -g @openai/codex` (requires `OPENAI_API_KEY`) **Usage:** ```bash # Implement a feature request pdd change https://github.com/owner/repo/issues/123 # Or fix a bug pdd bug https://github.com/owner/repo/issues/456 pdd fix https://github.com/owner/repo/issues/456 ``` ### Option 3: Manual Prompt Workflow For learning PDD fundamentals or working with existing prompt files: ```bash cd your-project pdd sync module_name # Full automated workflow ``` See the [Hello Example](#-quickstart-hello-example) below for a step-by-step introduction. --- ## 🚀 Quickstart (Hello Example) If you want to understand PDD fundamentals, follow this manual example to see it in action. 1. **Install prerequisites** (macOS/Linux): ```bash xcode-select --install # macOS only curl -LsSf https://astral.sh/uv/install.sh | sh uv tool install pdd-cli pdd --version ``` 2. **Clone repo** ```bash # Clone the repository (if not already done) git clone https://github.com/promptdriven/pdd.git cd pdd/examples/hello ``` 3. **Set one API key** (choose your provider): ```bash export GEMINI_API_KEY="your-gemini-key" # OR export OPENAI_API_KEY="your-openai-key" ``` ### Post-Installation Setup (Required first step after installation) Run the comprehensive setup wizard: ```bash pdd setup ``` The setup wizard runs these steps: 1. Detects agentic CLI tools (Claude, Gemini, Codex) and offers installation and API key configuration if needed 2. Scans for API keys across `.env`, and `~/.pdd/api-env.*`, and the shell environment; prompts to add one if none are found 3. Configures models from a reference CSV `data/llm_model.csv` of top models (ELO ≥ 1400) across all LiteLLM-supported providers based on your available keys 4. Optionally creates a `.pddrc` project config 5. Tests the first available model with a real LLM call 6. Prints a structured summary (CLIs, keys, models, test result) The wizard can be re-run at any time to update keys, add providers, or reconfigure settings. > **Important:** After setup completes, source the API environment file so your keys take effect in the current terminal session: > ```bash > source ~/.pdd/api-env.zsh # or api-env.bash, depending on your shell > ``` > New terminal windows will load keys automatically. If you skip this step, the first regular pdd command you run will detect the missing setup files and print a reminder banner so you can finish onboarding later. 5. **Run Hello**: ```bash cd ../hello pdd --force generate hello_python.prompt python3 hello.py ``` ✅ Expected output: ``` hello ``` ## Cloud vs Local Execution PDD commands can be run either in the cloud or locally. By default, all commands run in the cloud mode, which provides several advantages: - No need to manage API keys locally - Access to more powerful models - Shared examples and improvements across the PDD community - Automatic updates and improvements - Better cost optimization ### Cloud Authentication When running in cloud mode (default), PDD uses GitHub Single Sign-On (SSO) for authentication. On first use, you'll be prompted to authenticate: 1. PDD will open your default browser to the GitHub login page 2. Log in with your GitHub account 3. Authorize PDD Cloud to access your GitHub profile 4. Once authenticated, you can return to your terminal to continue using PDD The authentication token is securely stored locally and automatically refreshed as needed. ### Local Mode Requirements When running in local mode with the `--local` flag, you'll need to set up API keys for the language models: ```bash # For OpenAI export OPENAI_API_KEY=your_api_key_here # For Anthropic export ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=your_api_key_here # For other supported providers (LiteLLM supports multiple LLM providers) export PROVIDER_API_KEY=your_api_key_here ``` Add these to your `.bashrc`, `.zshrc`, or equivalent for persistence. PDD's local mode uses LiteLLM (version 1.75.5 or higher) for interacting with language models, providing: - Support for multiple model providers (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google/Vertex AI, and more) - Automatic model selection based on strength settings - Response caching for improved performance - Smart token usage tracking and cost estimation - Interactive API key acquisition when keys are missing When keys are missing, PDD will prompt for them interactively and securely store them in your local `.env` file. ### Local Model Configuration PDD uses a CSV file to configure model selection and capabilities. This configuration is loaded from: 1. User-specific configuration: `~/.pdd/llm_model.csv` (takes precedence if it exists) 2. Project-specific configuration: `/.pdd/llm_model.csv` 3. Package default: Bundled with PDD installation (fallback when no local configurations exist) The CSV includes columns for: - `provider`: The LLM provider (e.g., "openai", "anthropic", "google") - `model`: The LiteLLM model identifier (e.g., "gpt-4", "claude-3-opus-20240229") - `input`/`output`: Costs per million tokens - `coding_arena_elo`: ELO rating for coding ability - `api_key`: The environment variable name for the required API key - `structured_output`: Whether the model supports structured JSON output - `reasoning_type`: Support for reasoning capabilities ("none", "budget", or "effort") For a concrete, up-to-date reference of supported models and example rows, see the bundled CSV in this repository: [pdd/data/llm_model.csv](pdd/data/llm_model.csv). For proper model identifiers to use in your custom configuration, refer to the [LiteLLM Model List](https://docs.litellm.ai/docs/providers) documentation. LiteLLM typically uses model identifiers in the format `provider/model_name` (e.g., "openai/gpt-4", "anthropic/claude-3-opus-20240229"). ## Troubleshooting Common Installation Issues 1. **Command not found** ```bash # Add to PATH if needed export PATH="$HOME/.local/bin:$PATH" ``` 2. **Permission errors** ```bash # Install with user permissions pip install --user pdd-cli ``` 3. **macOS-s

items.title: Phase 5 foundation: architecture ↔ <include> validation, auto-deps arch updates, and non-silent sync-order divergence
pdd change: prompt file not committed despite being referenced in commit message
pdd bug: analysis should trace proposed fix forward to the user's symptom
pdd fix: tests must use the real artifact from the bug report, not synthetic stand-ins
deterministic grep verification of Step 6 FIX_LOCATIONS in orchestrator
pdd fix should have a clean up step

repo_cards.description: Next generation frontend tooling. It's fast!

readme.text: # Vite ⚡ > Next Generation Frontend Tooling - 💡 Instant Server Start - ⚡️ Lightning Fast HMR - 🛠️ Rich Features - 📦 Optimized Build - 🔩 Universal Plugin Interface - 🔑 Fully Typed APIs Vite (French word for "quick", pronounced [`/viːt/`](https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/gh/vitejs/vite@main/docs/public/vite.mp3), like "veet") is a new breed of frontend build tooling that significantly improves the frontend development experience. It consists of two major parts: - A dev server that serves your source files over [native ES modules](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/JavaScript/Guide/Modules), with [rich built-in features](https://vite.dev/guide/features.html) and astonishingly fast [Hot Module Replacement (HMR)](https://vite.dev/guide/features.html#hot-module-replacement). - A [build command](https://vite.dev/guide/build.html) that bundles your code with [Rollup](https://rollupjs.org), pre-configured to output highly optimized static assets for production. In addition, Vite is highly extensible via its [Plugin API](https://vite.dev/guide/api-plugin.html) and [JavaScript API](https://vite.dev/guide/api-javascript.html) with full typing support. [Read the Docs to Learn More](https://vite.dev). ## Packages | Package | Version (click for changelogs) | | ----------------------------------------------- | :-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | | [vite](packages/vite) | [![vite version](https://img.shields.io/npm/v/vite.svg?label=%20)](packages/vite/CHANGELOG.md) | | [@vitejs/plugin-legacy](packages/plugin-legacy) | [![plugin-legacy version](https://img.shields.io/npm/v/@vitejs/plugin-legacy.svg?label=%20)](packages/plugin-legacy/CHANGELOG.md) | | [create-vite](packages/create-vite) | [![create-vite version](https://img.shields.io/npm/v/create-vite.svg?label=%20)](packages/create-vite/CHANGELOG.md) | ## Contribution See [Contributing Guide](CONTRIBUTING.md). ## License [MIT](LICENSE). ## Sponsors

repo_cards.description: Your Unity project's AI autopilot. Compile, test, debug, repeat—until it just works.

readme.text:
# Unity CLI Loop [日本語](README_ja.md) [![Unity](https://img.shields.io/badge/Unity-2022.3+-red.svg)](https://unity3d.com/) [![License](https://img.shields.io/badge/license-MIT-green.svg)](LICENSE.md)
![ClaudeCode](https://img.shields.io/badge/Claude_Code-555?logo=claude) ![Cursor](https://img.shields.io/badge/Cursor-111?logo=Cursor) ![Codex](https://img.shields.io/badge/Codex-111?logo=data:image/svg+xml;base64,) ![Antigravity](https://img.shields.io/badge/Antigravity-111?logo=google) ![GitHubCopilot](https://img.shields.io/badge/GitHub_Copilot-111?logo=githubcopilot)

(Logo inspired by Daft Punk's Homework album art)

Let an AI agent compile, test, and operate your Unity project from popular LLM tools via CLI. Designed to keep AI-driven development loops running autonomously inside your existing Unity projects. > **Note**: This project was formerly known as **uLoopMCP**. # Concept Unity CLI Loop is a Unity integration tool designed so that **AI can drive your Unity project forward with minimal human intervention**. Tasks that humans typically handle manually—compiling, running the Test Runner, checking logs, editing scenes, and capturing windows to verify UI layouts—are exposed as tools that LLMs can orchestrate. Unity CLI Loop is built around four core ideas: 1. **A self-hosted development loop where AI autonomously compiles, tests, inspects logs, and fixes issues.** Uses `compile`, `run-tests`, `get-logs`, `clear-console`. 2. **AI-driven Unity Editor operation—scene building, object manipulation, menu execution, and UI refinement from screenshots.** Uses `execute-dynamic-code`, `execute-menu-item`, `screenshot`. 3. **PlayMode automated testing—AI clicks buttons, drags elements, presses keys, records and replays input, and verifies game behavior.** Uses `simulate-mouse-ui`, `simulate-mouse-input`, `simulate-keyboard`, `record-input`, `replay-input`, `execute-dynamic-code`, `screenshot`. 4. **Achieving the above with a minimal set of tools.** See [Design Philosophy](#design-philosophy). https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/569a2110-7351-4cf3-8281-3a83fe181817 # Installation > [!WARNING] > The following software is required > > - **Unity 2022.3 or later** > - **Node.js 22.0 or later** - Required for CLI execution > - Install via the [official site](https://nodejs.org/en/download) or your preferred version manager ## Via Unity Package Manager 1. Open Unity Editor 2. Open Window > Package Manager 3. Click the "+" button 4. Select "Add package from git URL" 5. Enter the following URL: ```text https://github.com/hatayama/unity-cli-loop.git?path=/Packages/src ``` > **If you installed via git URL before v1.0.0**: The repository was renamed from `uLoopMCP` to `unity-cli-loop` in v1.0.0. Please update your `manifest.json`: > ```text > Old: https://github.com/hatayama/uLoopMCP.git?path=/Packages/src > New: https://github.com/hatayama/unity-cli-loop.git?path=/Packages/src > ``` > The old URL still works via GitHub redirect, but updating is recommended. OpenUPM users are not affected. ## Via OpenUPM (Recommended) ## Using Scoped registry in Unity Package Manager 1. Open Project Settings window and go to Package Manager page 2. Add the following entry to the Scoped Registries list: ```text Name: OpenUPM URL: https://package.openupm.com Scope(s): io.github.hatayama.uloopmcp ``` 3. Open Package Manager window and select OpenUPM in the My Registries section. Unity CLI Loop will be displayed. # Quickstart ## Step 1: Install the CLI Select Window > Unity CLI Loop > Settings. A dedicated window will open — confirm that the **CLI** button is highlighted in blue. Press the **Install CLI** button. If you see the following display, the installation was successful.
To install from terminal ```bash npm install -g uloop-cli ``` See [uloop-cli on npm](https://www.npmjs.com/package/uloop-cli) for details.
## Step 2: Install Skills Select your target (Claude Code, Codex, etc.) and press the **Install Skills** button.
To install from terminal ```bash # Install for Claude Code project uloop skills install --claude # Install for OpenAI Codex project uloop skills install --codex # Or install globally uloop skills install --claude --global ```
That's it! After installing Skills, LLM tools can automatically handle instructions like these: | Your Instruction | Skill Used by LLM Tools | |---|---| | "Launch Unity for this project" | `/uloop-launch` | | "Fix the compile errors" | `/uloop-compile` | | "Run the tests and tell me why they failed" | `/uloop-run-tests` + `/uloop-get-logs` | | "Check the scene hierarchy" | `/uloop-get-hierarchy` | | "Play the game and bring Unity to the front" | `/uloop-control-play-mode` + `/uloop-focus-window` | | "Bulk-update prefab parameters" | `/uloop-execute-dynamic-code` | | "Take a screenshot of Game View and adjust the UI layout" | `/uloop-screenshot` + `/uloop-execute-dynamic-code` | | "Record my gameplay input" | `/uloop-record-input` | | "Replay the recorded input" | `/uloop-replay-input` |
All 17 Bundled Skills - `/uloop-launch` - Launch Unity with correct version - `/uloop-compile` - Execute compilation - `/uloop-get-logs` - Get console logs - `/uloop-run-tests` - Run tests - `/uloop-clear-console` - Clear console - `/uloop-focus-window` - Bring Unity Editor to front - `/uloop-get-hierarchy` - Get scene hierarchy - `/uloop-execute-menu-item` - Execute menu item - `/uloop-find-game-objects` - Find GameObjects - `/uloop-screenshot` - Capture EditorWindow - `/uloop-simulate-mouse-ui` - Simulate mouse click, long-press, and drag on PlayMode UI elements - `/uloop-simulate-mouse-input` - Simulate mouse input in PlayMode via Input System - `/uloop-simulate-keyboard` - Simulate keyboard input in PlayMode via Input System - `/uloop-record-input` - Record keyboard and mouse input during PlayMode - `/uloop-replay-input` - Replay recorded input during PlayMode - `/uloop-control-play-mode` - Control Play Mode - `/uloop-execute-dynamic-code` - Execute dynamic C# code
Direct CLI Usage (Advanced) You can also call the CLI directly without using Skills: ```bash # List available tools uloop list # Launch Unity project with correct version uloop launch # Launch with build target (Android, iOS, StandaloneOSX, etc.) uloop launch -p Android # Kill running Unity and restart uloop launch -r # Execute compilation uloop compile # Compile and wait for Domain Reload to complete uloop compile --wait-for-domain-reload true # Get logs uloop get-logs --max-count 10 # Run tests uloop run-tests --filter-type all # Execute dynamic code uloop execute-dynamic-code --code 'using UnityEngine; Debug.Log("Hello from CLI!");' ```
Shell Completion (Optional) You can install Bash/Zsh/PowerShell completion: ```bash # Add completion script to shell config (auto-detects shell) uloop completion --install # Explicitly specify shell (when auto-detection fails on Windows) uloop completion --shell bash --install # Git Bash / MINGW64 uloop completion --shell powershell --install # PowerShell # Check completion script uloop completion ```
## Project Path Specification If `--project-path` is omitted, the port is automatically selected from the Unity project detected in the current directory. To operate multiple Unity instances from a single LLM tool, explicitly specify a project path: ```bash # Specify by project path (absolute or relative) uloop compile --project-path /Users/foo/my-unity-project uloop compile --project-path ../other-project ```
Using MCP instead of CLI > [!WARNING] > MCP connection may be deprecated or removed in a future release. We recommend using the CLI instead. You can also connect via MCP (Model Context Protocol). CLI and Skills installation is not required for MCP. ### MCP Connection Steps 1. Select Window > Unity CLI Loop > Settings. A dedicated window will open — press the **MCP** button. 2. Next, select the target IDE in the LLM Tool Settings section. Press the yellow "Configure {LLM Tool Name}" button to automatically connect to the IDE. 3. IDE Connection Verification - For example, with Cursor, check the Tools & MCP in the settings page and find uLoopMCP. Click the toggle to enable MCP. > [!WARNING] > **About Windsurf** > Project-level configuration is not supported; only a global configuration is available.
Manual Setup (Usually Unnecessary) > [!NOTE] > Usually automatic setup is sufficient, but if needed, you can manually edit the configuration file (e.g., `mcp.json`): ```json { "mcpServers": { "uLoopMCP": { "command": "node", "args": [ "[Unity Package Path]/TypeScriptServer~/dist/server.bundle.js" ], "env": {

evidence.title: README (segment 2)
evidence.excerpt: (Logo inspired by Daft Punk's Homework album art)

evidence.title: README (segment 3)
evidence.excerpt: Let an AI agent compile, test, and operate your Unity project from popular LLM tools via CLI.

evidence.title: README (segment 4)
evidence.excerpt: Designed to keep AI-driven development loops running autonomously inside your existing Unity projects.

evidence.title: README (segment 5)
evidence.excerpt: # Concept Unity CLI Loop is a Unity integration tool designed so that **AI can drive your Unity project forward with minimal human intervention**. Tasks that humans typically handle manually—compiling, running the Test Runner, checking logs, editing scenes, and capturing windows to verify UI layouts—are exposed as tools that LLMs can orchestrate.

evidence.title: README (segment 6)
evidence.excerpt: 1. **A self-hosted development loop where AI autonomously compiles, tests, inspects logs, and fixes issues.** Uses `compile`, `run-tests`, `get-logs`, `clear-console`. 2. **AI-driven Unity Editor operation—scene building, object manipulation, menu execution, and UI refinement from screenshots.** Uses `execute-dynamic-code`, `execute-menu-item`, `screenshot`. 3. **PlayMode automated testing—AI clicks buttons, drags elements, presses keys, records and replays input, and verifies game behavior.** Uses `simulate-mouse-ui`, `simulate-mouse-input`, `simulate-keyboard`, `record-input`, `replay-input`, `execute-dynamic-code`, `screenshot`. 4. **Achieving the above with a minimal set of tools.** See [Design Philosophy](#design-philosophy).

evidence.title: README (segment 7)
evidence.excerpt: > [!WARNING] > The following software is required > > - **Unity 2022.3 or later** > - **Node.js 22.0 or later** - Required for CLI execution > - Install via the [official site](https://nodejs.org/en/download) or your preferred version manager

evidence.title: README (segment 8)
evidence.excerpt: 1. Open Unity Editor 2. Open Window > Package Manager 3. Click the "+" button 4. Select "Add package from git URL" 5. Enter the following URL: ```text https://github.com/hatayama/unity-cli-loop.git?path=/Packages/src ```

repo_cards.description: ☄️ Markdown notes that start private. Encrypted when they travel. Decentralized when you publish.

readme.text:
# comet

Comet is a local-first notes app built with Tauri, React, TypeScript, and Rust.

- Local-first note-taking
- Encrypted sync with Nostr
- Encrypted blob storage with Blossom
- Turborepo monorepo with the app in `app/`

## Quick Start

1. Install `just`: `brew install just` or `cargo install just`
2. Install dependencies: `just install`
3. Start the app: `just app-dev`

## Commands

The full command surface lives in [justfile](/Users/chris/Repos/project/comet/justfile). Use `just --list` to see everything.

Common commands:

- `just app-dev`: run the Comet app in development
- `just app-seed`: seed the app account with the fixture note set
- `just app-seed-account`: seed only the app account, without notes or attachments
- `just docs-dev`: run the standalone docs site in development
- `just docs-build`: build the standalone docs site
- `just docs-check`: run the standalone docs verification suite
- `just app-check`: run the main app verification suite
- `just relay-dev`: run the relay in development
- `just blossom-dev`: run the Blossom server in development
- `just relay-test`: run the relay test suite
- `just blossom-test`: run the Blossom test suite
- `just format-check`: check formatting across the repo

Comet keeps a root `app.db` plus per-account databases under `accounts/<npub>/comet.db`. The maintenance scripts and MCP server always resolve the active account through `app.db`.

## Seed Data

The app seed workflow uses a fixed seed identity from `app/.env` so repeated runs target the same account.

1. Copy [`app/.env.example`](/Users/chris/Repos/project/comet/app/.env.example) to `app/.env`
2. Set `COMET_SEED_NSEC` to the seed account secret you want to reuse
3. Run `just app-seed` for the full fixture dataset, or `just app-seed-account` to create only the seeded account workspace

`just app-seed-account` skips note fixtures, blob metadata, and attachment installation, but it still creates and activates the seeded account database.

## Repo Layout

- [`app`](/Users/chris/Repos/project/comet/app): the Comet app workspace
- [`app/src`](/Users/chris/Repos/project/comet/app/src): React frontend
- [`app/src-tauri`](/Users/chris/Repos/project/comet/app/src-tauri): Tauri + Rust backend
- [`blossom`](/Users/chris/Repos/project/comet/blossom): Bun-based Blossom server workspace
- [`docs`](/Users/chris/Repos/project/comet/docs): Astro Starlight docs site
- [`relay`](/Users/chris/Repos/project/comet/relay): Bun-based Nostr relay workspace
- [`packages/data`](/Users/chris/Repos/project/comet/packages/data): shared Postgres schema and migrations
- [`packages/nostr`](/Users/chris/Repos/project/comet/packages/nostr): shared Nostr validation/auth helpers

## Workspace

- Root scripts use Turborepo for workspace tasks like `build`, `lint`, `typecheck`, and `test`
- The app source, Vite config, and Tauri project live in [`app`](/Users/chris/Repos/project/comet/app)
- The docs source and Starlight content live in [`docs`](/Users/chris/Repos/project/comet/docs)

## Testing Notes

- `just app-test-frontend` runs the frontend test suite
- `just app-test-backend` runs the Rust test suite
- `just app-test` runs both

Relay and Blossom development use Bun. Install Bun if you plan to run commands under [`relay`](/Users/chris/Repos/project/comet/relay) or [`blossom`](/Users/chris/Repos/project/comet/blossom). The default app workflows such as `just app-build` and `just app-check` do not require Bun.

Relay and Blossom test suites use Postgres. Set `TEST_DATABASE_URL` or run a local `comet_test` database before `just relay-test` or `just blossom-test`.

Use a disposable local test database only. Relay and Blossom integration tests run migrations and truncate shared tables during setup, so do not point `TEST_DATABASE_URL` at a real app, staging, or production database.

### Local Postgres setup

One working local setup on macOS is:

```sh
brew install postgresql@16
brew services start postgresql@16
createdb comet_test
export TEST_DATABASE_URL=postgres://$USER@localhost:5432/comet_test
```

Then run:

```sh
just relay-test
just blossom-test
```

If you already have Postgres running, you usually only need:

```sh
createdb comet_test
export TEST_DATABASE_URL=postgres://$USER@localhost:5432/comet_test
```

## Git hooks

This repo uses Husky + lint-staged for a pre-commit hook on staged files. After `just install`, the hook is installed automatically via the `prepare` script.

On commit, staged files run through:

- `eslint --fix` and `prettier --write` for `*.ts` and `*.tsx`
- `prettier --write` for `*.js`, `*.mjs`, `*.cjs`, `*.json`, `*.md`, `*.yml`, and `*.yaml`

- Clamp gutter clicks to editor boundaries ([`d003e53`](https://github.com/nodetec/comet/commit/d003e53f6f21701d9ec9c9af1357e66723dcae75))

## Changes

scout.summary: The repository implements Prompt-Driven Development (PDD), an AI-powered toolkit for automated code generation, testing, and maintenance via CLI and web interface. It emphasizes agentic workflows tied to GitHub issues and includes sophisticated prompt-based synchronization and validation mechanisms.
highlights: Offers both a web UI (localhost:9876) and CLI with specialized commands like `pdd change`, `pdd bug`, and `pdd fix` that map directly to GitHub issue workflows.
Promotes real artifact usage in testing and traceable fix validation, as seen in closed issues addressing test fidelity and symptom tracing.
Includes advanced features like architecture ↔ <include> validation, auto-dependency updates, and deterministic verification of fix locations.
Provides extensive documentation including a whitepaper with benchmarks and setup guides for multiple environments (Gemini, Windows).
risks: High number of open issues (168) relative to closed ones may indicate unresolved complexity or scalability challenges in the core orchestration logic.
CLI version is still at v0.0.179, suggesting immaturity despite feature richness and active development.

inventor.summary: PDD introduces a novel development methodology—Prompt-Driven Development—that tightly integrates AI-generated code with issue-driven workflows, enabling automated implementation, testing, and architectural synchronization through both CLI and web interfaces.
highlights: Invents a structured 12–18 step agentic workflow (e.g., `pdd change`, `pdd test`) that maps GitHub issues directly to automated code generation, test creation, and validation cycles.
Pioneers bidirectional synchronization between architecture definitions and code includes, with auto-dependency updates and validation to prevent sync-order divergence.
Enforces traceability by requiring fixes to be validated against real user-reported artifacts and symptoms, not synthetic proxies.
Introduces deterministic verification (e.g., grep-based) of fix locations in orchestration pipelines to ensure precision in AI-applied changes.
risks: The invented workflows are highly complex (e.g., 18-step test generation), which may hinder adoption or introduce fragility if underlying assumptions about prompt stability or code structure shift.
Core innovation relies on tight coupling between prompts, architecture.json, and file includes; any drift or misalignment could cascade into silent failures despite validation efforts.

engineer.summary: PDD is an AI-assisted development framework that automates code generation, testing, and maintenance through tightly integrated CLI and web interfaces, using GitHub issues as the primary driver for agentic workflows with structured multi-step pipelines.
highlights: Implements deterministic, issue-driven automation via CLI commands (`pdd change`, `pdd bug`, `pdd fix`, etc.) that follow rigorous multi-step workflows (up to 18 steps) for generating code, tests, and architecture definitions.
Enforces fidelity in testing by mandating use of real user-reported artifacts instead of synthetic data, and requires forward-tracing of fixes to original symptoms.
Features bidirectional sync between architecture definitions and code includes, with validation mechanisms to detect and prevent silent divergence in dependency or sync order.
Provides a local web UI (localhost:9876) for visual project management alongside powerful prompt-based orchestration via the `sync` command.
risks: The system’s reliance on complex, multi-stage orchestration (e.g., Step 6 FIX_LOCATIONS verification) introduces potential fragility; even minor deviations in file structure or prompt output could break deterministic assumptions.
Despite closed issues addressing cleanup and validation, the high volume of open issues (168) suggests ongoing instability in core synchronization or dependency resolution logic.

skeptic.summary: Despite ambitious claims of AI-powered automation and rigorous workflows, PDD exhibits signs of immaturity, over-engineering, and unresolved core issues that cast doubt on its reliability and practicality for real-world use.
highlights: The CLI remains at v0.0.179 despite complex multi-step workflows (e.g., 18-step test generation), suggesting the tool is far from production-ready even after extensive development.
Closed issues reveal recurring problems with basic version control hygiene (e.g., uncommitted prompt files) and incomplete fix validation, undermining trust in automated changes.

The system enforces deterministic verification (e.g., grep-based FIX_LOCATIONS) yet still struggles with sync-order divergence and dependency management, indicating fundamental fragility in its orchestration model.
Heavy reliance on real artifacts and symptom tracing implies prior synthetic approaches failed—raising questions about whether current safeguards are sufficient or merely reactive patches.
risks: The high open issue count (168) relative to minimal releases suggests chronic instability; users risk adopting a system where core assumptions (e.g., prompt-code alignment) frequently break.
Complex agentic workflows may create an illusion of automation while masking manual intervention needs, especially when prompts or code structures deviate from expected patterns.

Bidirectional architecture ↔ include synchronization sounds robust in theory but has known failure modes (e.g., non-silent divergence), which could lead to undetected inconsistencies in generated codebases.

scout.summary: Vite is a modern, high-performance frontend build tool offering instant dev server start, fast HMR, and optimized production builds via Rollup. It emphasizes extensibility through a typed plugin and JavaScript API, with active maintenance and strong community support.
highlights: Core features include instant server start, lightning-fast HMR, and optimized Rollup-based builds [ID:E-RD-002][ID:E-RD-004][ID:E-RD-005].
Highly extensible with universal plugin interface and fully typed APIs [ID:E-RD-002][ID:E-RD-006].
Actively maintained with recent releases (v8.0.x series) and detailed changelogs [ID:E-RL-001][ID:E-RL-003].
Well-structured monorepo with clear documentation, contribution guidelines, and CI integration [ID:E-RF-001][ID:E-RD-001].
risks: Open issues indicate unresolved compatibility problems, such as CJS interop hoisting causing TDZ errors [ID:E-IS-001].
Tooling integration bugs persist, e.g., '$RefreshSig$ not defined' with pnpm + React setups [ID:E-IS-002].

inventor.summary: Vite is an innovative frontend build tool that reimagines development workflows by leveraging native ES modules for instant server start and ultra-fast HMR, while using Rollup for production-optimized bundling. Its architecture emphasizes extensibility through a universal plugin system and fully typed APIs, enabling rich customization without sacrificing performance or developer experience.
highlights: Introduces a dual-mode architecture: dev server using native ES modules with instant startup and HMR, and a Rollup-based production build pipeline [ID:E-RD-003][ID:E-RD-004][ID:E-RD-005].
Designed for extensibility via a universal plugin interface and fully typed JavaScript API, lowering integration barriers for tool authors [ID:E-RD-002][ID:E-RD-006].
Monorepo structure supports modular packages like vite, @vitejs/plugin-legacy, and create-vite, each with independent versioning and changelogs [ID:E-RD-007][ID:E-RL-001][ID:E-RL-002][ID:E-RL-003].
Strong foundational tooling: includes CI badges, contribution guidelines, and workspace configuration (pnpm), indicating mature engineering practices [ID:E-RF-001][ID:E-RD-001].
risks: Emerging interoperability gaps in module systems, such as CJS interop not being hoisted with imports, leading to TDZ errors—suggests edge cases in Vite’s module resolution innovation [ID:E-IS-001].
Integration fragility with package managers like pnpm in React Fast Refresh contexts ($RefreshSig$ undefined), revealing potential brittleness in runtime injection mechanisms [ID:E-IS-002].

engineer.summary: Vite is a high-performance frontend build tool built with TypeScript, featuring a dual architecture of an ES module-based dev server with instant startup and HMR, and a Rollup-powered production bundler. It offers strong extensibility via typed plugin and JavaScript APIs, follows a modular monorepo structure, and demonstrates mature engineering practices—but faces unresolved edge cases in CJS interop and pnpm/React integration.
highlights: Dual-mode architecture: dev server leverages native ES modules for instant start and fast HMR; production builds use pre-configured, optimized Rollup [ID:E-RD-004][ID:E-RD-005].
Extensible through a universal plugin interface and fully typed JavaScript API, enabling robust ecosystem integration [ID:E-RD-002][ID:E-RD-006].
Modular monorepo with independently versioned packages (vite, @vitejs/plugin-legacy, create-vite) and detailed per-package changelogs [ID:E-RD-007][ID:E-RL-001][ID:E-RL-002][ID:E-RL-003].
Mature project hygiene: includes CI workflow badges, contribution guidelines, pnpm workspace config, and standard tooling files [ID:E-RF-001][ID:E-RD-001].
risks: CJS interop assignment not hoisted with import statements can cause Temporal Dead Zone (TDZ) errors, indicating incomplete module resolution semantics [ID:E-IS-001].

Runtime injection fragility: '$RefreshSig$ is not defined' error in pnpm-managed React projects suggests instability in Fast Refresh integration under certain package manager configurations [ID:E-IS-002].

skeptic.summary: While Vite promotes itself as a fast, modern build tool with strong extensibility and performance, closer inspection reveals unresolved technical debt in module interoperability and ecosystem integration, casting doubt on its readiness for complex or edge-case production environments.
highlights: Claims of 'instant server start' and 'lightning fast HMR' are marketing-oriented and lack quantified benchmarks in the README [ID:E-RD-002].
Reliance on Rollup for production builds is noted, but no justification is provided for why this choice is optimal compared to alternatives like esbuild or Webpack [ID:E-RD-005].
Despite high star count and active releases, the project shows signs of fragility in real-world usage scenarios, particularly around module system interop [ID:E-IS-001].
risks: The CJS interop hoisting issue leading to TDZ errors suggests fundamental gaps in Vite’s module resolution logic, which could break applications relying on mixed ESM/CJS dependencies [ID:E-IS-001].

The '$RefreshSig$ not defined' error with pnpm and React indicates that Vite’s Fast Refresh implementation is not robust across common package manager configurations, undermining claims of universal compatibility [ID:E-IS-002].

Release notes consistently defer to CHANGELOG.md without summarizing critical fixes or breaking changes in release descriptions, reducing transparency for adopters [ID:E-RL-001][ID:E-RL-002][ID:E-RL-003].

scout.summary: The repository 'hatayama/unity-cli-loop' is an AI-driven automation tool for Unity projects, enabling autonomous compilation, testing, debugging, and UI interaction via CLI. It integrates with LLMs to orchestrate development tasks with minimal human intervention, targeting Unity 2022.3+ and requiring Node.js 22+. Recent releases focus on stability, port management, and preventing unwanted UI during package upgrades.
highlights: Enables AI agents to autonomously compile, test, inspect logs, and fix Unity projects via CLI tools.
Supports advanced AI-driven operations including scene manipulation, UI refinement via screenshots, and PlayMode input simulation.
Designed for minimal toolset efficiency while covering full development loop automation.
Actively maintained with recent bug fixes addressing MCP server recovery, port conflicts, and upgrade UX.
risks: Requires specific versions of Unity (2022.3+) and Node.js (22+), limiting compatibility with older projects.
Reliance on CLI-to-Unity communication via ports introduces potential connection instability if ports are stale or misconfigured.
Auto-generated release notes suggest heavy automation in CI/CD, which may obscure manual oversight of critical changes.

inventor.summary: Unity CLI Loop is an inventive framework that transforms Unity into an AI-controllable environment by exposing core editor and runtime operations as CLI tools for LLM orchestration, enabling autonomous development cycles.
highlights: Introduces a novel paradigm where LLMs drive Unity project iteration through a minimal set of CLI-exposed capabilities: compilation, testing, log inspection, scene manipulation, and UI interaction via screenshots.
Uniquely bridges Unity’s editor and PlayMode with external AI agents using dynamic code execution, menu item triggering, and input simulation—turning the engine into a programmable AI target.
Embeds self-healing mechanisms (e.g., MCP server recovery, port conflict prevention) to sustain long-running autonomous loops, a critical innovation for reliable AI-driven development.
Leverages Unity Package Manager for seamless integration, allowing AI automation to coexist within standard project workflows without invasive setup.
risks: The reliance on dynamic code execution and menu item invocation may expose Unity projects to unintended side effects or instability if AI-generated commands are malformed or unsafe.
Tight coupling with specific Unity (2022.3+) and Node.js (22+) versions could hinder adoption in legacy or constrained environments, limiting the invention's reach.
Autonomous operation assumes consistent tool behavior; changes in Unity’s internal APIs or CLI interface could break the AI loop without explicit version resilience.

engineer.summary: Unity CLI Loop is a C#-based Unity package that engineers can integrate to enable AI agents to control Unity projects via CLI, supporting autonomous compilation, testing, debugging, and UI interaction through a minimal set of well-defined tools.
highlights: Provides a structured CLI interface for AI agents to perform core Unity tasks: compile, run tests, inspect logs, manipulate scenes, simulate input, and capture screenshots (E-RD-006).
Integrates cleanly via Unity Package Manager using a git URL path, enabling non-intrusive adoption into existing projects (E-RD-008).
Recent engineering efforts focus on robustness: auto-recovery from MCP server exits, prevention of stale port connections, and suppression of unwanted UI during upgrades (E-RL-002, E-RL-003).
Enforces clear runtime dependencies—Unity 2022.3+ and Node.js 22+—ensuring compatibility with modern toolchains but limiting backward support (E-RD-007).
risks: Dynamic code execution and menu item invocation (E-RD-006) introduce potential instability if AI-generated commands trigger unsafe editor behavior.
Port-based communication between CLI and Unity Editor (E-RL-002) creates a fragile coupling that could fail under concurrent or misconfigured environments.
Heavy reliance on automated release pipelines (E-RL-001, E-RL-002) may reduce visibility into low-level changes affecting system stability.

skeptic.summary: Unity CLI Loop promises AI-driven autonomy for Unity development, but its practical reliability, security, and real-world utility remain questionable given its experimental tooling, fragile communication model, and lack of demonstrated robustness.
highlights: Claims of full AI autonomy rely on a narrow set of CLI tools that may not generalize across diverse Unity project structures or complex debugging scenarios.
The system depends on dynamic code execution and menu item invocation, which are inherently unsafe if driven by untrusted or hallucinated LLM outputs.
Port-based CLI-to-Unity communication is prone to race conditions and misidentification, as evidenced by repeated fixes for stale port connections and silent server exits.
risks: Dynamic code execution (E-RD-006) exposes projects to arbitrary code injection risks if AI agents generate malicious or malformed commands.
Frequent bug fixes around MCP server stability and port management (E-RL-002) suggest the underlying architecture is brittle and unsuitable for production use.
No evidence of comprehensive testing, error recovery beyond basic restarts, or safeguards against infinite loops or resource exhaustion during autonomous operation.
Heavy reliance on auto-generated releases (E-RL-001, E-RL-003) reduces transparency and increases risk of undetected regressions.

scout.summary: Comet is a local-first, encrypted note-taking app built on a Turborepo monorepo with integrated Nostr sync and Blossom blob storage. It emphasizes developer ergonomics via Just commands, supports deterministic seeding for testing, and maintains per-account SQLite databases. Recent releases show active iteration on editor UX and seeding workflows.
highlights: Local-first architecture with end-to-end encryption via Nostr and Blossom
Monorepo structure managed by Turborepo with dedicated workspaces for app, relay, Blossom, docs, and MCP
Rich Just-based CLI for development, testing, seeding, and formatting
Deterministic account seeding using fixed NSEC from .env enables repeatable test environments
Per-account SQLite databases (`accounts/<npub>/comet.db`) alongside a root `app.db` for state management
risks: Seeding relies on hardcoded secret in `.env`, which could leak if mismanaged [ID:E-RD-007]
Early-stage project (alpha releases only) with limited issue history despite open feature surface [ID:E-IS-001]
Complex multi-service architecture (app, relay, Blossom, MCP) may increase operational overhead for contributors

inventor.summary: Comet invents a privacy-centric note-taking system by combining local-first storage, Nostr-based encrypted sync, and Blossom for encrypted blob handling, all orchestrated through a modular Turborepo monorepo with deterministic developer workflows.
highlights: Invents a hybrid sync model: notes are private by default locally, then encrypted end-to-end when synced via Nostr and stored in Blossom
Introduces a deterministic seeding mechanism using a fixed NSEC in `.env` to enable repeatable account creation and testing [ID:E-RD-006][ID:E-RD-007][ID:E-RL-003]
Architects a multi-database SQLite strategy with a root `app.db` coordinating per-account databases under `accounts/<npub>/comet.db` for isolated, identity-scoped data [ID:E-RD-005]
Packages full development, testing, and deployment workflows into a unified Just command interface, abstracting complexity of the multi-service stack [ID:E-RD-002][ID:E-RD-004]
risks: The fixed seed secret approach for deterministic accounts creates a potential key leakage vector if `.env` is mishandled or committed [ID:E-RD-007]
Tight coupling between app, relay, and Blossom services may hinder independent evolution or third-party integration despite modular structure [ID:E-RF-001][ID:E-RD-001]
Encryption implementation details (e.g., key management, forward secrecy) are not visible in available evidence, raising auditability concerns [ID:E-IS-001]

engineer.summary: Comet is a TypeScript-based, local-first note-taking application built on a Turborepo monorepo, integrating Nostr for encrypted sync and Blossom for encrypted blob storage. It uses SQLite with per-account databases managed via a root app.db, and provides a comprehensive Just-based CLI for development, testing, and deterministic seeding using a fixed NSEC from .env.
highlights: Monorepo architecture powered by Turborepo with distinct workspaces for app, relay, Blossom, docs, and MCP services [ID:E-RF-001][ID:E-RD-001]
Deterministic developer workflows enabled by Just commands (e.g., `just app-seed`, `just relay-dev`) and fixed seed identity via `.env` [ID:E-RD-002][ID:E-RD-004][ID:E-RD-006][ID:E-RL-003]
Multi-database SQLite design: root `app.db` coordinates account-specific databases at `accounts/<npub>/comet.db` for isolated data per Nostr identity [ID:E-RD-005][ID:E-RD-008]
Active alpha development with recent commits refining editor UX (gutter clicks, module splitting) and seeding logic [ID:E-RL-001][ID:E-RL-002][ID:E-RL-003]
risks: Hardcoded seed secret (`COMET_SEED_NSEC`) in `.env` poses key leakage risk if accidentally committed or mishandled [ID:E-RD-007]
Lack of visible encryption implementation details (e.g., key derivation, rotation) despite closed issue on note encryption [ID:E-IS-001]
Operational complexity from tightly integrated multi-service stack (app, relay, Blossom) may hinder onboarding and independent deployment [ID:E-RD-001][ID:E-RF-001]

skeptic.summary: Comet claims end-to-end encrypted, local-first note-taking with Nostr and Blossom, but critical security and operational details are absent or rely on fragile assumptions, raising doubts about real-world privacy guarantees and maintainability.
highlights: Encryption claims lack implementation evidence—closed issue 'encrypt notes' [ID:E-IS-001] provides no proof of robust cryptographic design
Deterministic seeding depends on a hardcoded secret in `.env` [ID:E-RD-007], creating a single point of failure if exposed
Per-account SQLite databases assume secure isolation, yet no access control or encryption-at-rest mechanisms are described [ID:E-RD-005]
Active alpha releases focus on UX tweaks (e.g., gutter clicks) rather than foundational security or sync reliability [ID:E-RL-001][ID:E-RL-002]
risks: The fixed `COMET_SEED_NSEC` in `.env` could lead to account compromise if leaked, undermining the entire 'private by default' premise [ID:E-RD-007]
No visible audit trail or documentation for encryption protocols—claims of 'encrypted sync' may be superficial without key management transparency [ID:E-IS-001]
Tightly coupled monorepo with custom Just workflows increases barrier to independent verification or contribution [ID:E-RD-002][ID:E-RF-001]
Reliance on local SQLite files without clear backup, migration, or corruption recovery strategy risks data loss [ID:E-RD-005]
